cards.text: An {{c1::lvalue reference}} is declared using an ampersand ({{c2::&}}) in the type declaration: {{c3::int& ref = x;}}

cards.back: An lvalue reference is an alias for an existing lvalue (such as a variable). It acts as an alternative name for the object it references.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: ref is an alias for x. Assigning 6 to ref modifies x because they refer to the same object.

cards.text: References {{c1::must}} be initialized when defined. Once initialized, they {{c2::cannot be reseated}} (changed to reference another object).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Error 1: Reference not initialized. Error 2: Cannot bind lvalue reference to rvalue (5 is a literal/rvalue).
cards.explanation: References must be initialized when declared and can only be bound to lvalues, not rvalues.

cards.front: Can a reference be reseated to refer to a different object after initialization?
cards.back: No. Once a reference is bound to an object, it cannot be changed to reference a different object. This is different from pointers, which can be reassigned.

cards.question: What does this demonstrate about references?
cards.answer: Both output the same address, proving that ref is truly an alias for x, not a separate object.
cards.explanation: A reference has the same address as the object it references because it's an alias, not a copy.

Lvalue references have these important properties:
  • Must be initialized when declared
  • Cannot be reseated (bound to a different object)
  • Can only bind to lvalues (not rvalues)
  • Act as an alias with same address as referent
  • Automatically dereferenced (no * needed)
examples: int& ref = x;  // OK: initialized with lvalue
ref = 10;      // OK: modifies x through ref

cards.text: To declare multiple references, write {{c1::int& ref1, &ref2;}} or better, declare them on {{c2::separate lines}}.
